ORIGINAL ARTICLE

# Airway-centered Interstitial Fibrosis

# A Distinct Form of Aggressive Diffuse Lung Disease

Andrew Churg, MD,* Jeffrey Myers, MD,† Teresa Suarez, MD,‡ Miguel Gaxiola, MD,‡ Andrea Estrada, MD,‡ Mayra Mejia, MD,‡ and Moises Selman, MD‡

# Abstract:

We describe 12 patients with a form of interstitial lung disease characterized pathologically by small airway-centered interstitial fibrosis and metaplastic bronchiolar epithelium extending around and often linking fibrotic and sometimes heavily muscularized bronchioles. Clinically, patients presented with chronic cough and progressive dyspnea. One was a current light smoker and two were ex-smokers. In 8 patients, a history of possible inhalational exposures, including wood smoke, birds, cotton, pasture, chalk dust, agrochemical compounds, and cocaine use, was elicited. Pulmonary function tests showed moderate to severe physiologic abnormalities, in most instances indicating a restrictive lung disease with decreased peripheral flow rates. Chest radiographs revealed predominantly diffuse reticulonodular infiltrates in the central lung fields, with thickening of the bronchial walls and decreased lung volumes. Chest computed tomography demonstrated peribronchovascular fibrosis and interstitial thickening. Bronchoalveolar lavage showed a mild increase in lymphocytes in 4 subjects. Patients were treated with corticosteroids and bronchodilators. Follow-up data were available in 10 patients. In 5 patients, the disease progressed and 4 of them died. Two patients remained stable and 3 improved or healed. We propose that these findings represent a distinct airway-centered disease that mostly behaves as an interstitial lung disease and may exhibit a poor outcome.

Key Words: bronchiolitis, interstitial lung disease, small airway disease, fibrosis (Am J Surg Pathol 2004;28:62–68)

The idiopathic interstitial pneumonias consist of a set of diseases of unknown etiology characterized pathologically by variable combination of interstitial fibrosis and inflammation with, in some instances, airspace macrophages or granulation tissue. As a general rule, the inflammatory and fibrotic changes are widespread and variably affect bronchovascular bundles, alveolar septa, interlobular connective tissue septa, and occasionally visceral pleura. Most of these lesions demonstrate either a homogeneous distribution within the lobule or a tendency to peripheral accentuation, as in usual interstitial pneumonia.

# METHODS

# Study Population

Twelve patients with a distinctive histopathologic picture consisting of scarring and distortion of the bronchioles with interstitial fibrosis extending away from the affected airways were identified in a review of a series of patients with clinical interstitial lung disease from the National Institute of Respiratory Diseases in Mexico City. The pathology specimens were reviewed by two of us (A.C. and J.M.) without knowledge of the clinical details. The records of these 12 patients, including smoking and environmental exposure history, respiratory function tests, were analyzed.

From the *Department of Pathology, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada; †Department of Pathology, Mayo Clinic, Rochester, Minnesota; and ‡Instituto Nacional de Enfermedades Respiratorias, Mexico City, Mexico.

Airway-centered Interstitial Fibrosis

chest radiographs, chest computed tomography (CT, 5 patients), and bronchoalveolar lavage (7 patients), were reevaluated. Patients were followed from 1 to 10 years after diagnosis (mean 2.9 ± 2.6 years), and outcome was classified at last follow-up as resolved (disappearance of symptoms and normalization of the respiratory function tests), improved (higher 10% increase in forced vital capacity [FVC] plus higher 4 mm Hg increase in PaO₂), stable, worse (higher 10% decrease in FVC plus higher 4 mm Hg decrease in PaO₂), or dead of disease.

# RESULTS

# Histopathology

At low magnification, the key finding was a distinctive pattern of airway-centered interstitial fibrosis centered on membranous and respiratory bronchioles (Figs. 1–4). The bronchioles themselves were often narrowed and distorted (Figs. 1, 4) but were never overtly obstructed by either granulation tissue plugs or dense fibrosis. In some cases, subepithelial fibrosis was present in the bronchiolar wall, but more commonly the bronchiolar wall was greatly thickened by adventitial fibrous tissue; and in some instances, there was marked muscle hyperplasia (Figs. 1, 2). Although the fibrosing process primarily involved and extended away from the respiratory bronchioles; in many instances, the more proximal membranous bronchioles were abnormal as well, with increased peribronchiolar fibrous tissue (Figs. 2, 3) and sometimes increased bronchiolar muscle (Fig. 2) and occasionally a chronic inflammatory infiltrate in the airway wall.

Variable degrees of interstitial fibrosis extended away from the affected airways, and in many instances there was prominent bronchiolar metaplasia overlying the fibrotic alveolar walls (Figs. 1, 3, 5). In some cases, the fibrosing process appeared to link affected bronchioles (Fig. 1); in others, the metaplastic bronchiolar epithelium disappeared, but linear interstitial fibrosis extended considerable distances away from the airway. Interstitial inflammation was sparse in most cases but, when present, was characterized by small numbers of lymphocytes and plasma cells. The airspaces generally contained no or only small numbers of macrophages. Honeycombing was not present in the biopsy samples, and neither bronchiectasis nor bronchiolectasis was observed in the tissues. In some foci, the fibrosing process extended to the pleura and the pleura was often fibrotic as well (Figs. 1, 3, 4).

pages.md:
Churg et al    Am J Surg Pathol • Volume 28, Number 1, January 2004

# Demographic and Clinical Data

All patients presented with chronic cough and slowly progressive dyspnea, usually of more than 1 year in duration. There was a 2:1 female predominance, and the disease occurred usually between the ages of 40 and 65 years (Table 1).

Respiratory function tests showed a decreased FVC and forced expiratory volume in 1 second (FEV₁). However, FEV₁/FVC ratio was over 80% in 10 of 12 patients, suggesting a restrictive pattern (Table 1). Moreover, the residual volume was decreased or normal in all but two of the patients in which this test was performed. Peripheral flow rates were generally low. Almost all patients had some degree of respiratory insufficiency when first seen, with variable degrees of hypoxemia, and occasionally, with CO₂ retention.

Bronchoalveolar lavage was performed in 7 patients without remarkable findings. In 4 patients, a mild to moderate increase of lymphocytes was revealed (Table 1). In addition, bronchoalveolar lavage from 4 of the 7 patients showed from 1% to 4% eosinophils.

Chest radiographs were available for all patients and were always abnormal. The predominant pattern was diffuse reticulonodular infiltrates with central predominance, thickening of the bronchial walls, and small central ring shadows. Small lung fields were observed in 10 patients (Fig. 6). CT scans were available in 5 patients. The main abnormality was peribronchovascular interstitial thickening and traction bronchiectasis with thickened airway walls and surrounding fibrosis (Fig. 7A). Conglomerate fibrotic masses were present adjacent to central airways in some patients. Bronchioloectasis.

Moreover, the residual volume was decreased or normal in all but two of the patients in which this test was performed. Peripheral flow rates were generally low. Almost all patients had some degree of respiratory insufficiency when first seen, with variable degrees of hypoxemia, and occasionally, with CO₂ retention.

items.md: Bronchoalveolar lavage was performed in 7 patients without remarkable findings. In 4 patients, a mild to moderate increase of lymphocytes was revealed (Table 1). In addition, bronchoalveolar lavage from 4 of the 7 patients showed from 1% to 4% eosinophils.
items.value: Bronchoalveolar lavage was performed in 7 patients without remarkable findings. In 4 patients, a mild to moderate increase of lymphocytes was revealed (Table 1). In addition, bronchoalveolar lavage from 4 of the 7 patients showed from 1% to 4% eosinophils.

items.md: Chest radiographs were available for all patients and were always abnormal. The predominant pattern was diffuse reticulonodular infiltrates with central predominance, thickening of the bronchial walls, and small central ring shadows. Small lung fields were observed in 10 patients (Fig. 6). CT scans were available in 5 patients. The main abnormality was peribronchovascular interstitial thickening and traction bronchiectasis with thickened airway walls and surrounding fibrosis (Fig. 7A). Conglomerate fibrotic masses were present adjacent to central airways in some patients. Bronchioloectasis.

and/or honeycombing were present in three patients (Figs. 7B).

# Follow-up and Outcome

Only small areas of ground glass attenuation were seen. The first four patients who were seen (patient nos. 1–4 in Tables 1, 2) were treated with prednisone for at least for 1 year, starting with 1 mg/kg per day for 1 month followed by progressive reduction toward maintenance levels of 5 to 15 mg/day.

Eight patients had various environmental or occupational exposures that may have been linked to their respiratory syndromes (Table 2). Two of them had birds at home for several years, including the time period when they first noticed respiratory symptoms. These two patients had had other exposures in the past. One of them used an oil stove for cooking for many years, and the other used to work in a cotton (textile) factory. Two patients were exposed for many years to wood smoke, a known cause of chronic bronchitis. An additional patient used to inhale cocaine and was also a current, although minimal (1.5 packs/year), cigarette smoker. One patient was a teacher with a history of exposure to chalk dust for many years; she continued to be exposed at the time of presentation. Of note, she developed rheumatoid arthritis 8 years after the lung biopsy. Two patients were former light (1.25 and 5.25 packs/year) cigarette smokers. One of these latter two patients was also a gardener who was exposed to different agrochemical compounds, and the other was an agricultural worker exposed to dust from pastures. In 4 patients, no significant exposures were identified, although all 4 resided for many years in Mexico City, a region of high air pollution. Two additional patients had also lived in the Mexico City metropolitan area, while the other 6 lived in other regions of Mexico.

In 5 patients, the disease progressed despite treatment, and 4 of them died (1, 3, 3, and 10 years after diagnosis). Two patients remained stable, 3 improved or healed, while 1 was lost during early follow-up and 1 was only recently diagnosed. Four of 5 patients who worsened (patient no. 8) or died (patient nos. 4, 6, and 11) had more severe functional impairment at the time of diagnosis (Table 1).

# DISCUSSION

In this paper, we have presented 12 patients with clinical findings of an interstitial lung disease of restrictive type, but a histologic picture that combines a largely fibrotic bronchiolar disease with a variable degree of bronchiolocentric interstitial fibrosis. Because the CT scans show that there is considerable fibrosis around the larger airways as well, we propose that this lesion be called airway-centered interstitial fibrosis (ACIF). Whether ACIF is primarily a bronchitis/bronchiolitis or an alveolar process that secondarily affects the airways is unclear. It should be noted that we are using bronchiolitis here in

the broadest sense of the term since the lesions as seen at biopsy are not overtly inflammatory in most cases; rather, the small airways show considerable peri-airway fibrosis and increased muscle, both of which may be the residue of an inflammatory reaction that is no longer present (the same naming convention applies to “constrictive bronchiolitis,” see below). Moreover, the apparent airway centricity to the process and the quite variable degrees of interstitial fibrosis suggest that the airway lesions are the earliest manifestation of the disease. This conclusion is made with caution because the CT scans suggest that the disease process is often more severe than is reflected in the biopsies, probably because the lesions show a central rather than a peripheral distribution within the lung.

# Airway-centered Interstitial Fibrosis

# TABLE 2. Exposure to Noxious Agents, Therapy, and Outcome

| 3           | Wood-smoke, exposed at the beginning of symptoms; pigeons during 3 years until 10 years before the beginning of symptoms                                   | Prednisone                | Improved         |

that in fact there is often a true bronchiolitis and that this cor- and in all instances their total pack-years smoked was very low. RBILD typically shows centrilobular nodules and ground glass opacities on CT scan, although some cases show bron- chial wall thickening and peripheral ring shadows. Morpho- logically, RBILD shows much milder airway and interstitial changes than we observed and is always distinguished by the presence of pigmented (“smoker’s”) macrophages, but these characteristic cells laden with brown or dark pigmented cytoplasmic granules were not identified in our material. As well, our cases also lack the clinical and radiologic findings expected in HP. In most cases of HP, CT scans show a combination of ground glass opacities, poorly defined centrilobular micronodules, and air trapping with a mosaic pattern of attenuation, features not seen in the present cases. Only a few of our cases had potential antigenic exposures, and in the two patients with birds at home specific anti-avian antibodies were negative, a situation that is unusual in typical pigeon breeder’s disease. Lastly, bronchoalveolar lavage lymphocytosis is usually >40% in HP, and this was not observed in our patients. Therefore, we do not believe that our cases represent a variant of HP. Constrictive bronchiolitis (CBO), also referred to as obliterative bronchiolitis, shows perhaps the greatest degree of morphologic overlap with the features described in our patients. Pathologically, CBO shows thickened airway walls with a variable mixture of subepithelial fibrosis and muscle hyperplasia. Adventitial fibrosis can be present as well. Typically, in CBO there is either luminal obliteration or clear luminal.

Churg et al    Am J Surg Pathol • Volume 28, Number 1, January 2004

nal narrowing.7,8 Occasional examples of CBO with some degree of extension of the fibrosing process into the surrounding interstitium, accompanied by bronchiolar metaplasia of the overlying epithelium, have been reported,⁷ but the process has generally been mild and very focal. Although the histologic findings in CBO do show some overlap with the cases we describe here, the radiographic findings in our patients are not at all typical of CBO, which usually shows hyperinflation or increased bronchovascular markings. Abnormal small airway tests, such as those we observed, are common in a variety of forms of interstitial lung disease including HP, sarcoidosis, and even ideopathic pulmonary fibrosis (IPF),3,9,11,14 but most cases of CBO show clinically significant airflow limitation or gas trapping⁸ as opposed to the nearly uniform pattern of restriction seen in our patients.

Another form of predominantly centrilobular scarring has been described by De Carvalho et al.² These patients had very dense fibrosis that was confined to the centrilobular region and did not spread in the fashion seen our patients; in addition, the airways had necrotic epithelium in almost all their cases. The authors suggested that this process was secondary to aspiration. We did not observe necrotic epithelium, aspirated foreign material, or an acute inflammatory process in any of our cases, and we do not believe that our lesions are related to aspiration.

The histologic findings in our patients are similar in some respects to those recently described by Yousem and Dacic¹⁷ as “idiopathic bronchiolocentric interstitial pneumonia.” In both series, there is interstitial inflammation and fibrosis in a peribronchiolar distribution, often with bronchiolar metaplasia. Our patients differ mainly in the presence of prominent radiographic changes involving the airways and in the consistent histologic evidence of quite marked peribronchial fibrosis and increased bronchial muscle. This contrasts with the lack of both large and small airway disease on high resolution CT scans, and the apparently minimal morphologic small airway abnormalities in the 10 patients described by Yousem and Dacic.¹⁷ Similarities include a predilection for women, evidence of restriction and impaired gas exchange on pulmonary function studies, and an aggressive course in some patients; indeed, 33% of the patients reported by Yousem and Dacic died of their disease.¹⁷ Yousem and Dacic proposed that at least some of their patients may have had an unusual form of hypersensitivity pneumonitis (HP), but, as noted, we were unable to find any convincing support for a diagnosis of HP in our patients. Lesions similar to those described in our patients have also been observed by others but without systematic analysis. For example, Travis et al¹⁶ illustrate what may be a very mild variant of this lesion and comment that such patients may have clinical evidence of interstitial lung.

2. De Carvalho PEP, Kairalla RA, Capelozzi VL, et al. Centrilobular fibrosis: a novel histological pattern of idiopathic interstitial pneumonia. Pathol Res Pract. 2002;198:577–583.
3. Fulmer JD, Roberts WC, von Gal ER, et al. Small airways in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis: comparison of morphologic and physiologic observations. J Clin Invest. 1977;60:595–610.
4. Glazer CS, Rose CS, Lynch DA. Clinical and radiologic manifestations of hypersensitivity pneumonitis. J Thorac Imaging. 2002;17:261–272.
5. Katzenstein AL, Myers JL. Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis: clinical relevance of pathologic classification. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 1998;157:1301–1315.
6. King TE, Mortenson RL. Syndromes that mimic idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. Immunol Allergy Clin North Am. 1992;12:461–489.
7. Kraft M, Mortenson RL, Colby TV, et al. Cryptogenic constrictive bronchiolitis: a clinicopathologic study. Am Rev Respir Dis. 1993;148:1093–1101.
8. Markopoulou KD, Cool CD, Elliot TL, et al. Obliterative bronchiolitis: varying presentations and clinicopathological correlation. Eur Respir J. 2002;19:20–30.
9. Miller A, Teirstein AS, Jackler I, et al. Airway function in chronic pulmonary sarcoidosis with fibrosis. Am Rev Respir Dis. 1974;109:179–189.
10. Myers JL, Veal CF, Shin MS, et al. Respiratory bronchiolitis causing interstitial lung disease: a clinicopathologic study of six cases. Am Rev Respir Dis. 1987;135:880–884.

Lesions similar to those described in our patients have also been observed by others but without systematic analysis. For example, Travis et al¹⁶ illustrate what may be a very mild variant of this lesion and comment that such patients may have clinical evidence of interstitial lung.
